schools.summary: The two parties' unprecedented alliance; the 'sign from heaven' = a spectacular wonder like Samuel's thunder or Elijah's fire, beyond the earthly miracles already given.
Red sky allegorised: evening = blood of the first advent's passion, lowering morning = fire of the second advent's judgment; their blindness to the prophets condemns them.
Chrysostom: earthly signs for the first coming as physician, heavenly signs reserved for the second as judge; so 'He left them.'
The demand for a new sign is the posture of the 'Papists,' treating the gospel as unproven and despising the signs already given.
They sought a sign Satan could not counterfeit and were refused all but the sign of Jonah, the resurrection.

debates.question: Are the weather-sayings of vv.2-3 original to Matthew?

sides.position: Doubtful / likely not original (absent from Aleph, B)
Genuine, the omission being a later difficulty

outliers.note: Dwells on the hostile alliance, likening it to Herod and Pilate uniting to destroy Jesus.

schools.summary: A universal weather-proverb; Robertson notes the poetic word eudia and the textual doubt over vv.2-3; Ellicott finds the same redness both dawn and storm.
Nature's instructions suffice for reading the sky, so failure to read the kingdom's clearer signs proves voluntary malice, not mistake.
Lightfoot details Jewish weather-lore; the rare stugnazon = a lowering sky; yet they were blind to the eminent signs that Messiah's days had come.
Calvin: men sharpen their wits for earthly gain yet quench the heavenly light, deserving to perish for ingratitude; the verse forbids no weather-prediction.
The signs of the times are Christ's doctrine and miracles; the gloomy sky warns of the coming storm of wrath.
A doublet of Matthew 12:39; Mark adds that Jesus 'sighed deeply'; the one sign is Jonah's, i.e. the resurrection that removes the scandal of the cross.
'Adulterous' = spiritual adultery: Israel, wedded to God, has forsaken Him for the world; love of the world blinds them.
Condemned not for asking a sign but for deliberately tempting God; the risen Christ will make His gospel heard everywhere.
Origen allegorises the adultery (the soul forsaking the law-husband for sin) and argues Jonah's sign is itself heavenly, being the resurrection.

outliers.note: Reads the soul as wife of the law-husband (Romans 7); the abandoned law makes them adulterous; Jonah's sign is heavenly.

schools.summary: Leaven = false doctrine that spreads from a spark to a flame (1 Cor 5:6); the heresies of Marcion and Valentinus; the disciples' affectionate poverty.
Chrysostom: Christ veiled the warning to recall the loaves to forgetful minds, rebuking them mildly to draw them off Jewish observance and little faith.
Calvin harmonises Herod/hypocrisy and applies it to a corrupting 'Theology of the Court'; keep a perpetual Passover of sincerity (1 Cor 5:8).
Narrative detail: the crossing, the single loaf (Mark); the warning grows from the disciples' perplexed looks.
Henry: disciples are in most danger from hypocrites, against whom they least stand guard.
Origen: the crossing is a passage from material to spiritual things, requiring a different bread.

outliers.note: Reads the crossing to 'the other side' as the soul's passage from sensible to intellectual things.

schools.summary: The disciples missed the metaphor because 'leaven' rarely meant doctrine among the Jews; Lightfoot on the rabbinic leaven-rules; Ellicott harmonises the 'leaven of Herod.'
The leaven is false doctrine — pride, hypocrisy, worldly-mindedness — that pervades soul and Church as leaven pervades meal.
The disciples' artless misreading marks the record's truthfulness; Robertson on the imperfect 'kept reasoning'; Clarke on how readily bodily wants are felt over the soul's.
Origen turns the rebuke inward: sin reasoned 'in the heart only' is really, if proportionately, punished.

outliers.note: Develops a doctrine of inward sin (adultery, theft, false witness 'in the heart only') from the rebuke on inward reasoning.

schools.summary: He read their thoughts unheard (omniscience); Robertson on the four pungent questions; Ellicott: He reproves not their dullness but their unbelief, since memory should have aided faith.
Calvin: to be anxious after two miraculous feedings is shameful ingratitude; distrust after experiencing God's power convicts of unbelief, for faith remembers God's gifts.
Clarke: little faith is the seed of great faith, not to be despised; Wesley: 'Am I not able to supply you by a word?'
'Understand' points to the disciples' failure to reckon with the power shown in the feeding of the five thousand (Matthew 14:19).
Clarke: men feel bodily wants quickly but forget mercies as fast; God lets them want, then supplies, so the gift is seen as His.
A bare cross-reference to the second feeding (Matthew 15:34); the seven loaves of the four thousand are the second proof of Christ's provision.
Ellicott: Christ does not interpret the parable but suggests the clue, and memory at last quickens the disciples' faith.
Clarke: ignorance comes from not improving our advantages, not from want of them; the leaven is Pharisaic hypocrisy and Sadducean denial.
Origen: while Jesus is with us (Matthew 28:20) we cannot be so starved as to crave the forbidden leaven.

outliers.note: Reads the abiding presence of Christ (Matthew 28:20) as the believer's bread against the forbidden leaven.

schools.summary: Robertson on the aorist 'they grasped it'; Barnes on the five ways doctrine resembles leaven; Ellicott (from Luke 12:1) names the leaven as hypocrisy and Sadducean worldliness.
Calvin: leaven = every foreign admixture to God's pure word; faith is spoiled when drawn from the simplicity of Christ (2 Cor 11:3); applied against Rome.

outliers.note: Comments on the parallel Nazareth scene ('Is not this the carpenter?'), reading it as evidence of Christ's real humanity.

schools.summary: At a remote place Christ lays the gospel's foundations, clearing away error; 'Son of Man' puts forward His lowly humanity that disciples may confess His deity.
Gill/Lightfoot on the city's history; 'Son of Man' = the promised seed of Adam, the second Adam (Genesis 3:15; Daniel 7:13), correcting earthly-Messiah dreams.
Chrysostom: He led them far from Judea so they might speak freely, and asked the common people's view, free of Pharisaic malice.
Origen: we should always learn what men think of us, to cut off occasions of evil and multiply occasions of good.
The opinions (John, Elijah, Jeremiah) are honourable but false guesses, all wide of the truth (Calvin, Wesley, Henry).

outliers.note: Reads the scene as Christ building a 'second Holy Church' of Christians out of the rejected synagogue.

schools.summary: Each guess traced: Herod's theory (Matthew 14:2) gave John; Malachi 4:5 and Elijah's wonders gave Elijah; Jeremiah as weeping prophet or set over nations (Jeremiah 1:10).
Henry: the opinions are honourable yet false — a high view of Christ, but not high enough; men would make Him anything but Messiah.
A people esteeming antiquity above present grace, unwilling to own a living prophet of their own age.
The emphatic 'ye' sets the disciples over against the crowd; their intimacy with Christ obliges a clearer knowledge.
Henry: greater means oblige clearer knowledge, and teachers must be examined on their view of Christ; the question presses every heart — is He precious to us?
Calvin: the question demands a settled personal confession, distinct from the floating popular opinions.
Peter speaks for all; the confession joins the Messianic title to true deity; 'the living God' set against dead idols, and his faith becomes the rock of v.18.
Calvin: the confession comprehends Christ's whole power — eternal Son, the fountain of life.
Origen: believing Christ is less than knowing Him; the apostles now rise to the higher, Father-revealed knowledge of the Son of the living God.

outliers.note: Distinguishes believing from knowing; the apostles ascend by stages to the revealed knowledge of the divine Son.

schools.summary: On 'Bar-jona' and the contrast of flesh-and-blood with the Father's revelation; Robertson: the Father revealed one truth, Jesus now reveals another.
Henry: revealed religion and saving faith are God's gift, a token of His good will; he warns against the pride lurking in Peter (1 Cor 4:7).
Calvin: the gifts are ascribed to faith as though a reward.
Jerome reads 'Bar-jona' (son of a dove) as the Holy Spirit's grace by which, not the Pharisees' teaching, Christ was revealed.

outliers.note: Exalts the apostles above the prophets, citing the rapture to the third heaven (2 Corinthians 12:2).

schools.summary: Peter is the rock, the foundation of unity and (in the medieval/interpolated stream) a single Vicar of Christ to whom the church has recourse.
The rock is Christ / Peter's confession, not the man; the apostles found the church together (Eph 2:20); Rome's claim refuted by Acts 15 and Galatians 2:11.
Ellicott weighs petros/petra and the OT 'Rock' for God (Deut 32:4), favouring Christ; Barnes grants Peter the rock in honour only, denying papal supremacy and succession.
Origen: 'every one is a Rock who imitates Christ'; the promise and keys belong to all the perfect, not Peter alone.

debates.question: What is the rock on which the church is built?

sides.position: Peter himself — grounding papal primacy and a single Vicar of Christ
Peter as honoured foundation-layer, but no supremacy or succession
Christ Himself, or Peter's confession of Him

outliers.note: Makes the rock and the keys common to every imitator of Christ, dissolving any unique Petrine office.

schools.summary: The key = steward's office (Isaiah 22:22) and scribe's key of knowledge (Luke 11:52); binding/loosing = rabbinic forbid/permit, of things not persons (Acts 15:20), not priestly absolution.
Calvin: ministers are 'porters of the kingdom'; the power is common to all apostles (Matthew 18:18); a long refutation of Petrine supremacy and succession.
Wesley: the keys were given to Peter first but not to him alone, equally to all (John 20:23).
Origen: the keys belong to 'every Peter,' each virtue opening its own gate of the kingdom.

debates.question: Do the keys and binding/loosing establish a unique Petrine (papal) authority?

sides.position: No — given to Peter first as spokesman, but common to all the apostles and untransmissible
Yes — Peter's singular receipt marks his supremacy
Tends toward the priestly power to remit sins

outliers.note: Universalises the keys to every believer, each virtue being its own key and gate.

schools.summary: A strict charge; the people would take 'Christ' politically (Robertson), and the hour had not come to provoke Jewish malice or Roman envy; Christ shunned crowd-kingship (John 6:15).
Calvin: the disciples were not yet ready to testify; their early apostleship was preparatory; silence until He is owned conqueror of death.
Origen: useless to preach Christ while silent on the cross; the full proclamation waits for the crucified and risen Lord; note the word 'show.'
Jerome distinguishes preaching 'Christ' from 'Jesus the Christ'; proclaim Him after the passion.

outliers.note: Stresses that the proclamation of Christ is defective until it includes the cross; dwells on the verb 'show.'

schools.summary: A dated new stage (~6 months before the cross); plainly named place, agents, death, third-day rising; Ellicott: its originality argues against a prophecy-after-the-event.
Henry: Christ reveals gradually; three aims — to show the eternal counsel, correct dreams of pomp, and forewarn for their own suffering; He must die at Jerusalem of the sacrifices.
From vague to express speech; the divine 'must' of the passion.
Origen: 'a prophet cannot perish out of Jerusalem'; He goes up to make the first-fruits of the resurrection.

outliers.note: Reads the necessity of Jerusalem allegorically — the place of the prophet's death and of the resurrection's first-fruits.

schools.summary: The words are an abbreviated prayer ('God be merciful to thee' / 'pity thyself'); Peter takes Him aside familiarly; he fastened on the death and missed the resurrection.
Jerome and Hilary excuse Peter's hot zeal and refuse to call it a devil's suggestion, imputing 'Satan' to the temptation, not the man.
Chrysostom: lacking revelation here, Peter judged suffering 'mean and unworthy' by human principles.
Calvin: real but presumptuous zeal that judges God by its own fancy; defer to Christ's mind over Peter's zeal.
Origen: Peter, forgetting the Son does nothing blameworthy, treated Him as one needing propitiation.

debates.question: Was Peter's protest a satanic suggestion or only an error of love?

sides.position: Only mistaken love, not the devil's prompting
A real temptation through Peter, judged severely

schools.summary: 'Satan' = adversary, not the devil; the rebuke is real but milder than it sounds; Peter, lately a rock, is now Satan's unwitting catspaw; the wilderness temptation renewed.
Origen: 'behind me' (not 'hence') recalls Peter to following (Matthew 4:19); the perfect cannot be offended, yet the stumbling-stone is real.
Calvin: a warning against the wisdom of the flesh, bruised with an iron hammer; restraining Peter restrains all from their own passions.
Wesley: the sharpness was needful against Peter's pride, puffed up by the recent commendation.

outliers.note: Distinguishes 'get behind me' (a call to follow) from the 'get hence' said to Satan; probes how the Lord can be 'offended.'

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the call is free, not compelled ('If any man will'), addressed to all ranks; self-denial means treating one's own body as a disowned stranger, even to the cross.
Gregory: to deny self is to become a new man and cease to be the old; even abstinence and compassion breed faults that 'follow me' alone purges; cross = crucified to the world.
Origen ties self-denial to dying with Christ (Galatians 2:20) and specifically to the cross (Galatians 6:14).
Henry: come after Christ, not prescribe to Him; self-denial is the strait gate; take up the appointed cross and improve it.
The saving and losing are weighed across death: a temporal life preserved by denial forfeits the eternal; a life lost for Christ is found everlasting.
Henry: apostasy to save life is self-destruction (saved 'but for a moment'); the martyrs despised valuable lives for Christ's testimony (Revelation 20:4).
Origen: 'losing the life' is either literal martyrdom or the surrender of carnal pleasures, per the two senses of self-denial.
Calvin: the paradox set in the balance of this life and the eternal.

outliers.note: Keeps the two senses (bodily death vs mortified flesh) parallel to his reading of self-denial in v.24.

schools.summary: 'Soul' = the same word as 'life' in v.25; the saying rises from the truism that gain helps no dead man to the soul's immortal, uncompensated loss.
Henry: four points — every man has a soul, it can be lost, by his own fault, and is worth more than the world ('Tekel,' Daniel 5:27).
Gregory, Jerome and others on the 'exchange': nothing, not the whole world, can ransom a soul once lost.
Origen presses the soul's incomparable value as made in God's image.
The final advent in the Father's glory with the angels; reward 'according to works' as evidence and measure, not the meritorious cause.
Calvin: the certain coming kingdom is the consolation that steadies the disciples against the lowliness of the cross.
Wesley: plain reward and accountability; the Son comes in the Father's majesty with the holy angels.
Origen reads the glory and the reward 'according to works' inwardly, of the soul's deeds and progress toward God.

outliers.note: Marshals apostolic texts on the Son's session at the right hand to confirm the coming Judge is the Sufferer in the Father's glory.

schools.summary: Robertson holds the range open (Transfiguration, Resurrection, Pentecost, fall of Jerusalem, or Second Coming), certain only of Christ's victory typified in various ways.
Calvin: the 'coming in his kingdom' is the glory begun at the resurrection and unfolded at Pentecost and in the miracles; the John-theory is 'ridiculous.'
Wesley and Gill: the mediatorial kingdom set up in power — Pentecost and the destruction of Jerusalem within that generation.
Origen reads it inwardly, of the soul beholding the kingdom in the Word; Cyril anchors it in the session at the right hand.

debates.question: What is the 'coming in his kingdom' that some would see before death?

sides.position: The Transfiguration (placed next in all three Gospels)
The resurrection, Pentecost, and the miracles — the glory of the new heavenly life
The mediatorial kingdom in power, issuing in the destruction of Jerusalem
Deliberately left open / typified in several ways

outliers.note: Reads the 'tasting no death' inwardly, of the soul that comes to behold the kingdom in the Word.
